---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## The Fixed SEED and `run_all`

- Part 10 — the fixed seed, `run_all`, and the honesty test

::: notes
Welcome back to The Eight-Minute Presentation and the Replication Packet, part ten of twelve. In part nine we covered the first four parts of the packet. Now the two parts this chapter makes non-negotiable: a fixed random seed, and a single run-all entry point that takes the whole pipeline from raw bytes to a finished PDF in one command.
:::

## A Fixed, Named SEED

- Any randomness seeded from one named constant, set once
- Bootstrap, train/test split, permutation, simulation
- Without it, your headline interval differs every run
- A reviewer gets a different interval — and doubts everything
- Recorded in the README: fixed *before* results, not tuned after

::: notes
The fifth part is a fixed random seed, and this chapter makes it non-negotiable. Anywhere your code uses randomness — a bootstrap, a train-test split, a permutation test, a simulation — it must be seeded from a single, named constant, set once at the top of the run, not scattered ad hoc. Without a fixed seed, your bootstrap confidence interval is different every run, so your headline interval is, strictly, irreproducible: a reviewer gets one-point-two to two-point-four where your paper said one-point-three to two-point-three, and now they doubt everything, fairly. With a fixed seed, the random parts come out identical, every time, on every machine. In modern NumPy you set one named constant — say the conference date — and build a seeded generator from it, then thread that generator through every draw rather than a global. The discipline is one seed, set once, recorded in the README so a reviewer knows it was fixed before results, not tuned after. A seed you searched over for a prettier interval is p-hacking with extra steps.
:::

## A Single `run_all` Entry Point

- One command runs the whole pipeline *in order*
- Pull, build, estimate, regenerate figures and tables, compile PDF
- A fresh clone: raw inputs to PDF, no human
- Figures and tables are *generated by code*, never pasted
- A pasted number silently drifts out of sync with the code

::: notes
The sixth part is a single make or run-all entry point — this is the one-click. One command runs the whole pipeline in order: pull or load cached, build the dataset, run every estimation, regenerate every figure and table, and compile the paper to P-D-F, so a fresh clone goes from raw inputs to finished document with no human in the loop. A Makefile and a shell script are equivalent; pick one, and have the shell script stop on the first error so it never silently half-builds. The non-negotiable property: the figures and tables in your paper are generated by the code, never pasted by hand. The analysis script writes the event-study figure and the main-results table, and the LaTeX includes and inputs them. The instant a human copies a number from a notebook into the manuscript, the chain breaks, and the pasted number can silently drift out of sync with the code that produced it.
:::

## The Honesty Test

- `make clean && make all`
- Delete every derived file, rebuild from nothing
- Paper comes back identical? The packet is real
- It doesn't? You just found a hand-edit
- And you found it *before* a reviewer did

::: notes
Here is the test that proves the whole thing. It is make clean, then make all: delete every derived file — every processed dataset, every figure, every table, the compiled P-D-F — and rebuild from nothing. If the paper comes back identical, the packet is real and the chain from raw data to finished document holds. If it does not, you just found a hand-edit somewhere — a number someone typed by hand, a figure exported once and forgotten — and you found it before a reviewer did. That is the value of the test: it turns a silent failure a skeptic would discover into a loud, private one you fix in advance. The packet that survives make clean and make all is the one you can hand to anyone.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The last two parts make the packet trustworthy: a **fixed, named SEED** set once and threaded through every random step — so a bootstrap interval is identical on every machine, fixed *before* results not tuned after — and a single **`run_all`** that rebuilds every figure and table from raw to PDF. Figures are *generated, never pasted*. The honesty test is **`make clean && make all`**: rebuild identical, or you found a hand-edit before a reviewer did. Next: why reproducibility *is* the credibility.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part ten: the two non-negotiable parts are the fixed seed and the single entry point. The seed is one named constant, set once and threaded through every random step, recorded as fixed before results — without it your bootstrap interval differs every run and a reviewer fairly doubts everything. The entry point is one command that runs pull, build, estimate, and compile in order, so a fresh clone goes from raw bytes to P-D-F with no human in the loop, every figure generated by code, never pasted. And the honesty test is make clean then make all: if the paper is not identical, you found a hand-edit before a reviewer did. In part eleven, why reproducibility is the credibility.
:::
